title: WinEvent Windows Task Scheduler Event Action Started
dataSources: Windows Event Log TaskScheduler 200, Windows Event Log TaskScheduler 201
spl: `wineventlog_task_scheduler` EventCode IN ("200","201")  | stats count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime by TaskName dvc EventCode | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)` | `winevent_windows_task_scheduler_event_action_started_filter`
description: The following analytic detects the execution of tasks registered in Windows Task Scheduler by monitoring EventID 200 (action run) and 201 (action completed) from the Task Scheduler logs. This detection leverages Task Scheduler logs to identify potentially suspicious or unauthorized task executions. Monitoring these events is significant for a SOC as it helps uncover evasive techniques used for persistence, unauthorized code execution, or other malicious activities. If confirmed malicious, this a…

implementation: Task Scheduler logs are required to be collected. Enable logging with inputs.conf by adding a stanza for [WinEventLog://Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler/Operational] and renderXml=false. Note, not translating it in XML may require a proper extraction of specific items in the Message.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable. No accelerated CIM datamodel tstats is implied by the main SPL; tune at the index and sourcetype level unless you add CIM normalization.
knownFalsePositives: Known-good admin tasks, change windows, and vendor agents can look suspicious out of context. Use scoped allowlists, asset context, and change-ticket checks before opening a high-severity incident.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"WinEvent Windows Task Scheduler Event Action Started" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Windows Event Log TaskScheduler 200, Windows Event Log TaskScheduler 201. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1053.005. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "WinEvent Windows Task Scheduler Event Action Started" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: Known-good admin tasks, change windows, and vendor agents can look suspicious out of context. Use scoped allowlists, asset context, and change-ticket checks before opening a high-severity incident.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

**Validate in production**

• Compare a sample of events with the source-of-truth console for the data source (cloud admin portal, device UI, or directory) covering the same time range.
• If you route findings through Enterprise Security, check the **Incident Review** queue and correlation search health so notables and drilldowns line up with what you see in search.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on the execution of tasks registered in Windows Task Scheduler by monitoring EventID 200 (action run) and 201 (action completed) from the Task Scheduler logs so unusual activity is visible early — before it spreads or hides in normal noise.
